```{r heterogeneity analysis, echo=T, message=F}
        Crop = .y$Product.Simple,
        k = mod$k,
        Effect = as.numeric(coef(mod)),
        SE = as.numeric(mod$se),
        CI_lower = as.numeric(mod$ci.lb),
        CI_upper = as.numeric(mod$ci.ub),
        tau2 = as.numeric(mod$tau2),
        I2 = as.numeric(mod$I2),
        Q = as.numeric(mod$QE),
        Q_pval = as.numeric(mod$QEp)
      )
    }
  }) %>%
  bind_rows()

fwrite(het_by_crop, file.path(pub_dir, "table_S4_heterogeneity_by_crop.csv"))

# Forest plot by crop
forest_data <- het_by_crop %>%
  mutate(
    Crop = reorder(Crop, Effect),
    label = sprintf("%.2f [%.2f, %.2f]", Effect, CI_lower, CI_upper)
  )

forest_plot <- ggplot(forest_data, aes(x = Effect, y = Crop)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey40") +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = CI_lower, xmax = CI_upper), height = 0.2) +
  geom_point(size = 4, shape = 18) +
  geom_text(aes(label = label, x = CI_upper + 0.1), hjust = 0, size = 3.5) +
  labs(
    title = "Overall Effect Size by Crop",
    subtitle = "Random effects meta-analysis",
    x = "Effect Size (lnRR)",
    y = NULL
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(min(forest_data$CI_lower) - 0.1,
                           max(forest_data$CI_upper) + 0.3),
                  clip = "off") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    plot.margin = margin(10, 60, 10, 10)
  )

print(forest_plot)

ggsave(
  file.path(pub_dir, "figure_S3_forest_plot_by_crop.png"),
  plot = forest_plot,
  width = 12, height = 8, dpi = 300, bg = "white"
)

cat("✓ Heterogeneity analysis complete\n\n")

```

# NEW SECTION: Paper Structure & Summary

```{r paper structure, echo=T, message=F}

cat("=== CREATING PUBLICATION PACKAGE SUMMARY ===\n\n")

# Create structured outline
outline <- "
RECOMMENDED PAPER STRUCTURE
===========================

TITLE: Climate-Smart Agriculture in Latin America: A Meta-Analysis of Yield 
       Responses to Management Practices Under Climate Stress

1. INTRODUCTION
   - Evidence synthesis of climate-agriculture interactions in LAC region
   - Gap: Limited quantitative synthesis for region-specific climate hazards
   - Objectives: 
     (1) Map available evidence across crops, practices, and geographies
     (2) Quantify yield responses to agricultural practices
     (3) Assess practice effectiveness under climate stress
     (4) Identify research gaps

2. METHODS
   2.1 Data Sources
       - ERA database (Evidence for Resilient Agriculture)
       - Sample: [INSERT N] studies, [INSERT N] observations
       - Geographic scope: LAC countries (focus on Dry Corridor)
       - Temporal coverage: [INSERT YEAR RANGE]
       - Target crops: Maize, Common Bean, Coffee
   
   2.2 Climate Variables
       - Source: ERA climate data pipeline
       - Variables: Rainfall balance, GDD above/below optimum, temperature
       - Units: Percentage change (rainfall), degree-days (GDD), °C (temperature)
       - Interpretation: Policy-relevant units for stakeholder communication
   
   2.3 Statistical Analysis
       - Effect size: Log response ratio (lnRR)
       - Three-level random effects meta-regression
       - Missing variance: Nakagawa CV imputation method
       - Model comparison: AIC, marginal R², p-values
       - Software: R (metafor, lme4, lmerTest packages)
   
   2.4 Data Quality & Limitations
       - Uneven geographic coverage (concentrated in [REGIONS])
       - Partial system effects for intercropping/rotation
       - Publication bias assessment (Egger's test)
       - Climate data coverage: [INSERT %]

3. RESULTS
   3.1 Evidence Map (Figure 1, Tables 1-3, S1-S3)
       - Geographic distribution
       - Temporal trends
       - Data quality assessment
       - Coverage by crop × country × practice
   
   3.2 Overall Practice Effects (Figure 2, Table 4)
       - Yield responses by practice
       - Crop-specific effects
       - Heterogeneity analysis
   
   3.3 Climate Predictor Comparison (Figure S1, Table 2)
       - Ranking of [INSERT N] climate variables
       - Best predictor: [INSERT VARIABLE]
       - Marginal R² and model fit statistics
   
   3.4 Management × Climate Interactions (Figures 3-5, Table 3, Table S5)
       - Rainfall sensitivity by practice
       - Heat stress (GDD above optimum) effects
       - Cold stress (GDD below optimum) effects
       - Practices with strongest climate buffering
   
   3.5 Publication Bias (Figure S2)
       - Funnel plot asymmetry
       - Egger's test results
       - Implications for synthesis

4. DISCUSSION
   4.1 Key Findings
       - [Practice X] shows strongest yield benefits (XX% increase)
       - Climate buffering: [Practice Y] most resilient to [climate stress Z]
       - Geographic gaps: Limited evidence from [regions]
   
   4.2 Implications for Climate-Smart Agriculture
       - Practice recommendations by climate zone
       - Scalability considerations
       - Co-benefits and trade-offs
   
   4.3 Limitations & Caveats
       - System-level metrics needed for intercropping/rotation
       - Missing economic analysis (requires cost data)
       - Limited soil health indicators
       - Temporal dynamics not captured
   
   4.4 Future Research Priorities
       - System-wide metrics: Land Equivalent Ratio (LER)
       - Economic returns and profitability
       - Long-term soil health outcomes
       - Farmer adoption barriers
       - Geographic expansion: [specific countries/regions]

5. CONCLUSIONS
   - Evidence synthesis demonstrates [key message]
   - Climate-smart practices can buffer against [specific hazards]
   - Critical gaps remain in [areas]
   - Recommendations for research and policy

SUPPLEMENTARY MATERIALS
========================
- Table S1: Data quality assessment by practice
- Table S2: Geographic coverage summary
- Table S3: System-level practice caveats
- Table S4: Heterogeneity analysis by crop
- Table S5: Full climate variable comparison
- Figure S1: Climate variable ranking
- Figure S2: Funnel plot (publication bias)
- Figure S3: Forest plot by crop
- Supplementary Methods: Detailed statistical procedures
- Data Availability Statement
- Code Repository: GitHub link

TARGET JOURNALS (Ranked)
========================
1. Regional Environmental Change (Impact Factor: ~4.5)
   - Focus: Climate adaptation in specific regions
   - Audience: Regional climate policy + research

2. Climate Risk Management (Open Access)
   - Focus: Climate adaptation strategies
   - Audience: Practitioners + policymakers

3. Agricultural Systems (Impact Factor: ~6.0)
   - Focus: Farming systems under global change
   - Audience: Agricultural research community

4. Agriculture, Ecosystems & Environment (Impact Factor: ~6.5)
   - Focus: Sustainable agriculture
   - Audience: Broad ecological + agricultural

5. Environmental Research Letters (Impact Factor: ~6.0)
   - Format: Concise, high-impact letters
   - Audience: Broad environmental science

ALTERNATIVE: If journal publication not feasible
================================================
- CGSpace Technical Report (extended)
- Format: 30-50 pages with full appendices
- Emphasis: Evidence gaps and research priorities
- Dissemination: CGIAR networks, regional workshops
"

writeLines(outline, file.path(pub_dir, "00_PAPER_STRUCTURE.txt"))

# Create README for publication package
readme <- "
PUBLICATION PACKAGE CONTENTS
============================

This directory contains all files needed for manuscript preparation.

MAIN TABLES
-----------
table1_data_coverage_by_crop.csv     - Overall data coverage
table2_climate_predictors_top20.csv  - Top climate variables (AIC, R², p)
table3_rainfall_sensitivity_by_practice.csv - Management × rainfall effects

SUPPLEMENTARY TABLES
--------------------
table_S1_data_quality_assessment.csv - Quality metrics by practice
table_S2_geographic_coverage.csv     - Coverage by country
table_S3_system_level_caveats.csv    - Practices needing system metrics
table_S4_heterogeneity_by_crop.csv   - I², tau², Q statistics
table2_climate_variable_comparison_full.csv - All climate variables tested

MAIN FIGURES
------------
figure3_rainfall_sensitivity.png     - Management × rainfall
figure4_heat_stress_sensitivity.png  - Management × heat (GDD above)
figure5_temperature_stress_panel.png - Combined temp stress effects

SUPPLEMENTARY FIGURES
---------------------
figure_S1_climate_variable_ranking.png - Climate predictor comparison
figure_S2_funnel_plot.png             - Publication bias assessment
figure_S3_forest_plot_by_crop.png     - Overall effects by crop

OTHER FILES
-----------
unmatched_climate_keys.csv           - Climate data merge diagnostics
00_PAPER_STRUCTURE.txt               - Recommended manuscript structure
README.txt                           - This file

SUGGESTED WORKFLOW
------------------
1. Review data quality tables (S1-S3)
2. Examine main results (Tables 1-3, Figures 3-5)
3. Check supplementary analyses (S1-S3)
4. Use paper structure outline for manuscript drafting
5. Cite specific table/figure numbers in text

CONTACT
-------
For questions about analysis or data, contact: [INSERT]
"

writeLines(readme, file.path(pub_dir, "README.txt"))

cat("✓ Publication package complete!\n\n")
cat("====================================\n")
cat("SUMMARY OF OUTPUTS\n")
cat("====================================\n")
cat("Location:", pub_dir, "\n\n")

# Count files
n_tables <- length(list.files(pub_dir, pattern = "\\.csv$"))
n_figures <- length(list.files(pub_dir, pattern = "\\.png$"))
n_docs <- length(list.files(pub_dir, pattern = "\\.txt$"))

cat("Generated files:\n")
cat("  -", n_tables, "data tables (CSV)\n")
cat("  -", n_figures, "figures (PNG, 300 DPI)\n")
cat("  -", n_docs, "documentation files\n\n")

cat("Next steps:\n")
cat("1. Review README.txt in publication package\n")
cat("2. Examine data quality tables\n")
cat("3. Use paper structure outline for writing\n")
cat("4. Select target journal from recommended list\n\n")

cat("✓ Analysis complete!\n")

```
